content: This systematic review evaluates the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting (IF) in adults with metabolic syndrome. Across multiple independent meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials, IF significantly reduced fasting blood glucose (pooled SMD = -0.51, 95% CI -0.81 to -0.20, p = 0.001) [7], systolic blood pressure by 5.54 mmHg (95% CI -7.55 to -3.53, p < 0.0001) [3], LDL cholesterol by 56.22 mg/dL (95% CI -80.14 to -32.29, p < 0.0001) [3], and body weight by approximately 3.59 kg in metabolic syndrome populations [3]. GRADE certainty was HIGH for several glycemic and lipid endpoints in the flagship 10-RCT / 701-participant meta-analysis [7]. The long-term cardiovascular safety profile remains contested: the 2024 American Heart Association NHANES analysis reported a 91% higher cardiovascular mortality with <8h eating windows, but methodological critiques regarding two-day dietary recall, reverse causation, and the predominantly pre-IF-adoption NHANES cohort temper the signal. Free-living Ramadan observational evidence shows no comparable mortality elevation. Major evidence gaps include long-duration RCTs beyond 6 months, low- and middle-income-country trials, head-to-head comparisons with GLP-1 receptor agonists, and human mechanistic studies of circadian and autophagy pathways. IF is a defensible first-line lifestyle intervention for appropriately selected adults with metabolic syndrome.
